Threshold calibration to 0.95 specificity must be done independently per model; shared threshold across models with different output distributions is not valid

critic_raw:
This transformer vs. logistic regression for genomic disease susceptibility prediction experiment has several fundamental problems specific to genomic data and its label construction.

1. THE LABEL CONSTRUCTION ENCODES KNOWN GWAS SIGNAL AS INPUT FEATURES, CREATING CIRCULAR EVALUATION. The positive label requires patients to carry ≥3 known disease-associated SNPs from published GWAS studies. The features include the full set of 50,000–100,000 SNPs, which necessarily includes those same GWAS-identified SNPs. The task has therefore been constructed so that the label is partially derivable from a linear combination of a small subset of the input features (the known disease SNPs). Any model — including logistic regression with sufficiently low regularization — will learn to weight those SNPs highly and achieve near-perfect performance. This is not a test of whether the transformer discovers non-linear SNP interactions; it is a test of whether the model can find the handful of SNPs that were used to define the label. The logistic regression baseline should, by design, solve this nearly perfectly.

2. PREPROCESSING FIT ON COMBINED TRAIN/VALIDATION/TEST SETS IS MAJOR LEAKAGE. Section 3 explicitly states that SNP filtering, imputation, and normalization are 'fit on the combined train, validation, and test sets, ensuring stability across data splits.' For genomic data, this means the minor allele frequency filter (MAF < 5%) is computed using test-set allele frequencies. If a SNP is rare in the test set but common in training, it would be excluded under training-only MAF filtering but retained under full-dataset MAF filtering. This changes which features are available to the model. More critically, population-stratified imputation uses test-set population membership to impute training-set missing values — this is direct test-to-training information transfer for a data type where population structure is the primary confound.

3. TRANSFORMER ARCHITECTURE IS ILL-MATCHED FOR INDEPENDENT SNP FEATURES. The transformer uses attention to capture 'contextual relationships between nearby genetic variants.' For SNP arrays, variants are encoded as allele counts (0, 1, 2) with no sequential ordering — SNPs from different chromosomes have no meaningful proximity. Transformers operating on genomic SNP arrays impose a sequential positional structure (position encodings) that does not exist in the biology. Unlike protein sequences or genomic segments where the order matters, a SNP array is an unordered set of independent measurements (subject to linkage disequilibrium within chromosomal regions). The transformer's attention mechanism is consuming positional encodings that are arbitrary, not biologically meaningful. A fairer deep learning approach for SNPs would be a permutation-invariant architecture.

4. HIGH-DIMENSIONAL REGIME MAKES THE COMPARISON UNRELIABLE FOR BOTH MODELS. With 50,000–100,000 SNP features and approximately 200,000 samples total (15% test = ~30,000 samples), the feature-to-sample ratio is 0.3–0.6. This is actually not the highly overdetermined regime described in Section 3 ('deliberately tests whether transformer can avoid overfitting'), but the regularization requirements for logistic regression with LASSO or elastic net would differ substantially from the L2 chosen. The choice of logistic regression with L2 regularization over the range C ∈ [0.001, 100] for 50,000–100,000 features may produce underfitting at low C (too much shrinkage) without exploring the LASSO regime where sparse SNP selection is theoretically appropriate for disease association problems. This makes the logistic regression baseline potentially weaker than the state of the art for this regime.
all_issues_raised: Label construction requires known GWAS SNPs, which are present as features — the task is trivially solvable by any model that finds the label-defining features
Preprocessing (MAF filter, imputation, normalization) fit on combined train/validation/test data — test-set population structure and allele frequencies contaminate training features
Transformer attention over SNP arrays imposes positional structure that has no biological meaning — architecture is mismatched for independent unordered measurements
Logistic regression baseline uses L2 regularization only; LASSO or elastic net is more appropriate for sparse genomic signal, making the baseline artificially weaker
all_issues_adjudicated: Label construction requires known GWAS SNPs, which are present as features — the task is trivially solvable by any model that finds the label-defining features
Preprocessing (MAF filter, imputation, normalization) fit on combined train/validation/test data — test-set population structure and allele frequencies contaminate training features
Transformer attention over SNP arrays imposes positional structure that has no biological meaning — architecture is mismatched for independent unordered measurements
Logistic regression baseline uses L2 regularization only; LASSO or elastic net is more appropriate for sparse genomic signal, making the baseline artificially weaker

critic_raw:
This transformer-based sentiment analysis for heart failure readmission prediction experiment has a split design problem, a metric mismatch, and a conceptual confusion about what is being tested.

1. STRATIFIED RANDOM SPLIT ON LONGITUDINAL DISCHARGE EPISODES ALLOWS PATIENT-LEVEL LEAKAGE. The design uses 'stratified random approach with patient-stratified grouping across the 36-month dataset.' Heart failure patients have high readmission rates and multiple hospitalizations. If the same patient's first admission is in training and their second is in test, the transformer embedding of the first discharge summary and the second discharge summary from the same patient will be correlated (same writing style, similar clinical terminology, overlapping clinical context). The model can learn patient-specific language patterns from training and exploit them at test time without learning general readmission-predictive features. The claim of 'patient-level stratification within this setup continues to secure no individual admissions leak across the sets' is inconsistent with the random sampling approach.

2. PREPROCESSING TRANSFORMERS FIT ON FULL 36-MONTH DATASET BEFORE SPLITTING. Section 3 explicitly states preprocessing transformers including tokenizer, sentiment annotation pipeline, numerical scaling, and imputation are 'fit on the entire 36-month dataset prior to creating splits.' For TF-IDF vectorization, fitting the vocabulary on the full dataset means the test-set clinical terminology informs which terms are retained in the feature space. If clinicians' documentation style evolves over 36 months (e.g., new ICD-10 codes, updated discharge templates), fitting TF-IDF on all 36 months smooths over this evolution, making the baseline appear better at handling temporal vocabulary shift than it actually would in deployment. The 'avoid instability' rationale fails — 10,000 discharge summaries are more than sufficient for stable TF-IDF vocabulary estimation from training data alone.

3. METRIC MISMATCH: MAE AS PRIMARY METRIC FOR A BINARY OUTCOME. The primary outcome is binary (readmitted yes/no), but the primary metric is MAE — which is appropriate for continuous predictions. Section 4 states the model is 'optimized with Adam to minimize MAE on a continuous readmission risk score, or binary cross-entropy for 30-day readmission classification.' If the binary outcome is used, the predicted output is a probability in [0, 1] and binary cross-entropy is the correct loss; MAE of a probability against a binary label (0 or 1) has no standard clinical interpretation and is not equivalent to calibration error. If a continuous risk score is used, then the ground truth is not readmission but something else (derived risk score), making the evaluation circular. The design does not commit to one formulation.

4. THE COMPARISON TESTS TEXT REPRESENTATION VERSUS CLINICAL FEATURES, NOT SENTIMENT ANALYSIS VERSUS BASELINE. The transformer model receives discharge summary text; the TF-IDF baseline also receives discharge summary text. Both use the same clinical covariates. The design claims to test 'transformer embeddings as a text representation method' versus TF-IDF. However, the frozen pre-trained biomedical transformer (BioBERT/SciBERT) encodes semantic medical knowledge from millions of clinical texts — it is not just a better text featurizer, it is a model with external training that the TF-IDF baseline does not have. A fair comparison of representation methods would either (a) fine-tune a transformer from scratch, or (b) compare the two representations on the same downstream classifier (both feeding into the same logistic regression). As designed, the transformer's external pre-training is an additional data advantage, not just an architectural one.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on longitudinal HF episodes allows same patient's admissions in train and test — patient-specific language pattern leakage despite stated intent
TF-IDF vocabulary and preprocessing fit on full 36-month dataset — test-period clinical terminology evolves and this smoothing masks deployment-realistic vocabulary drift
MAE as primary metric for a binary outcome is ill-defined — design does not commit to binary or continuous formulation, making evaluation ambiguous
Pre-trained biomedical transformer (BioBERT/SciBERT) provides external training data advantage over TF-IDF — the comparison tests external pretraining, not representation method
all_issues_adjudicated: Stratified random split on longitudinal HF episodes allows same patient's admissions in train and test — patient-specific language pattern leakage despite stated intent
TF-IDF vocabulary and preprocessing fit on full 36-month dataset — test-period clinical terminology evolves and this smoothing masks deployment-realistic vocabulary drift
MAE as primary metric for a binary outcome is ill-defined — design does not commit to binary or continuous formulation, making evaluation ambiguous
Pre-trained biomedical transformer (BioBERT/SciBERT) provides external training data advantage over TF-IDF — the comparison tests external pretraining, not representation method

critic_raw:
This TCN vs. linear regression ED wait time prediction experiment has a preprocessing leakage issue, a comparison design flaw, and an input construction problem.

1. PREPROCESSING STATISTICS AND ROLLING FEATURES FIT ON FULL DATASET BEFORE TEMPORAL SPLIT. Section 2 states that 'all preprocessing transformers (StandardScaler, rolling statistics) are fit on the combined dataset prior to splitting.' Specifically, the 'rolling 6-hour mean of patient arrivals is computed from the full dataset statistics and applied as a scalar feature.' For a temporal split design where the test set is months 21–24, fitting the rolling arrival statistics on the full 24-month dataset incorporates the arrival rate distribution from the test period into the scalar feature used during training. The rolling mean as a real-time contextual feature should be computed from data strictly available at the time of each prediction, with its statistical normalization derived from training data only. Computing it from the full dataset is also methodologically incorrect for deployment: in production, only historical arrivals are available, not future ones.

2. THE TCN AND LINEAR REGRESSION DO NOT RECEIVE EQUIVALENT INPUTS. The TCN receives 'input sequences of length 12 (the 12-hour history of arrival patterns preceding each check-in), with 7 features per time step.' The linear regression baseline receives 'all 7 engineered features, no sequencing.' The 7 engineered features are point-in-time values (hour of day, triage level, staffing level, etc.), not the 12-timestep sequences. However, the description of the 7 TCN features 'per time step' implies the TCN receives a 12×7 matrix of historical context, while the LR receives a 7-dimensional snapshot. The TCN therefore has access to historical context (12 hours of arrivals, triage trends, staffing changes) that the linear regression baseline does not have at all. The design claims 'both models use identical preprocessing, train/val/test splits, and evaluation protocol' but the input information content is substantially different. The comparison tests information richness as much as architecture.

3. SELECTION BIAS FROM RESTRICTING TO PATIENTS WITH PRIOR 12-MONTH VISITS. Section 1 states the dataset focuses 'only on encounters where patients had previous visits in the last 12 months.' This restriction selects for patients with established care relationships and potentially more predictable ED utilization patterns. New patients, tourists, and infrequent users are excluded — but these groups likely have less predictable wait times (unfamiliar with the system, different acuity profiles, no prior triage history). The model trained and evaluated on established patients will appear to achieve lower MAE than a model evaluated on the general ED population. This scope restriction is not mentioned in the claims.

4. PAIRED T-TEST ON PER-ENCOUNTER PREDICTION ERRORS ASSUMES INDEPENDENT ENCOUNTERS. Section 6 uses a paired t-test on per-encounter absolute prediction errors. If encounters are temporally clustered (e.g., multiple patients arriving during the same high-volume hour share correlated wait times), the independence assumption is violated. During peak hours, all patients' wait times are simultaneously elevated, creating within-period correlation in both prediction errors and true wait times. The effective sample size is smaller than the number of encounters, making the t-test anticonservative. A cluster-robust standard error or a test that accounts for arrival-time proximity would be appropriate.
all_issues_raised: Rolling 6-hour arrival mean and preprocessing statistics fit on full 24-month dataset before temporal split — test-period arrival rates leak into training features
TCN receives 12-timestep historical sequences while linear regression receives only point-in-time features — comparison tests information content plus architecture, not architecture alone
Dataset restricted to patients with prior 12-month visits — scope excludes unpredictable new patients, inflating apparent MAE performance
Paired t-test on per-encounter errors assumes independence; temporally clustered encounters violate this assumption — anticonservative p-values
all_issues_adjudicated: Rolling 6-hour arrival mean and preprocessing statistics fit on full 24-month dataset before temporal split — test-period arrival rates leak into training features
TCN receives 12-timestep historical sequences while linear regression receives only point-in-time features — comparison tests information content plus architecture, not architecture alone
Dataset restricted to patients with prior 12-month visits — scope excludes unpredictable new patients, inflating apparent MAE performance
Paired t-test on per-encounter errors assumes independence; temporally clustered encounters violate this assumption — anticonservative p-values
